description: This is the standard Maven Wrapper downloader boilerplate (MavenWrapperDownloader.java), a build-time bootstrap utility that runs locally during `mvn` invocation, not code deployed as part of the running application. The printStackTrace() call in the catch block has no untrusted-input reachability or production exposure; it's diagnostic output for a build tool, not active debug code left in a deployed app.

This is JUnit integration test code (src/it/java) that prints a stack trace before calling fail() on an unexpected exception during test execution; it is not part of deployed application logic reachable from untrusted input, so it does not expose sensitive information to end users.

This is test code in src/it/java (integration test source), not deployed application code; e.printStackTrace() in a JUnit test catch block followed by fail() is a standard test-failure diagnostic pattern, not active debug code reachable in a shipped application.

This is JUnit integration test helper code (src/it/java) that loads a local properties file and prints a stack trace on failure; there is no untrusted input reaching this path and no production entry point exposes it to attackers.

This is a hardcoded test fixture in a Playwright UI test that decodes the well-known jwt.io example JWT to verify the WebGoat JWT-decoding page renders header/payload correctly; it's not a live credential or secret used for authentication.

The flagged method only reads and returns the current enabled/disabled status (no state mutation), so even though the HTTP method is unrestricted, there's no state-changing action for CSRF to exploit; the actual mutating endpoint (setting the enabled flag) would need separate scrutiny.

setDebuggingStatus performs a state change (labelDebugger.setEnabled) but is mapped with @RequestMapping lacking a method restriction, so it is reachable via GET/HEAD without CSRF protection, matching the rule's exact concern.

The endpoint only reads and returns the lesson menu (no state mutation), so even though RequestMapping lacks an explicit method, there's no state-changing action for CSRF to exploit; this is a generic lint match rather than a genuine CSRF vulnerability.

The endpoint lacks an explicit HTTP method restriction, but the only state-changing logic (toggleSecurity/restartLesson) is commented out with a TODO, so the handler currently just returns a static message and performs no state change reachable via CSRF.

A static PINCODE value intended to act as a secret/challenge token is generated with java.util.Random(), which is not cryptographically secure and is predictable/seedable, matching the weak-random CWE-330 pattern exactly as described by the rule.

User-controlled password_login (and username_login) is concatenated directly into the SQL string rather than bound via PreparedStatement placeholders/setString, making the query trivially injectable (e.g., password_login = "' OR '1'='1"). The username check only gates on 'Larry' but does not prevent injection through either parameter.

The SQL query string is built via direct concatenation of untrusted user input (username_login, password_login) into the query text before being passed to prepareStatement, so no parameterization/binding occurs despite using the PreparedStatement API — this is classic SQL injection, and it's the intended vulnerable challenge in WebGoat's SQL injection lesson.

java.util.Random() is used to select an index into a secrets/password array whose value becomes the actual Basic Auth credential stored in the session, so a non-cryptographic PRNG is directly used to select an authentication secret, matching the rule's intent.

The value stored via setAttribute is basicAuth, a Base64-encoded string built from the authenticated principal's username and a server-selected random password from a fixed internal secrets array, not raw untrusted HTTP request data; there's no attacker-controlled input flowing into the session attribute here, so this is a mischaracterized taint flow rather than a real trust boundary violation.

The endpoint just computes and returns an MD5 hash for a WebGoat lesson display (GET-style behavior); it performs no privileged state change that would matter if forged via CSRF, so the missing explicit HTTP method is a generic lint match rather than an exploitable CSRF vector.

This is WebGoat, an intentionally-vulnerable training app; Random() here just selects an index into a small hardcoded array of demo secrets for a lesson about weak hashing (MD5), not to generate a cryptographic token, password, or session secret that provides real security guarantees.

The code explicitly instantiates MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5") and uses it to hash a secret value exposed via a REST endpoint, which is a genuine use of the broken MD5 algorithm matching the rule's detection criteria. This is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable teaching example for this exact CWE class, so the finding accurately reflects the code's actual behavior even though it's deliberate lesson content rather than an accidental bug.

This WebGoat lesson endpoint only computes/caches a hash value into the session for display purposes; it performs no sensitive state-changing action on behalf of the user that a CSRF attack would meaningfully exploit, so the missing explicit HTTP method is a style/hardening issue rather than a real CSRF vulnerability.

This is a WebGoat teaching lesson that selects an index into a small hardcoded SECRETS word list to demonstrate hash-cracking, not a cryptographic secret/token generator; predictability of the Random() selection adds no exploitable risk since the secrets are already a fixed, guessable dictionary by design of the exercise.

Although the handler literally lacks an explicit HTTP method (matching the rule pattern), it performs no security-relevant state change exploitable via CSRF — it only idempotently generates/caches a key pair in the user's own session and returns it in the response body, which an attacker cannot read cross-origin; this is WebGoat lesson code for the crypto-signing exercise, not a genuine CSRF-exploitable action.

The code deserializes a Base64-decoded byte stream directly from the untrusted `token` request parameter via ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no HMAC/signature verification or allow-listing before deserialization, matching CWE-502; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson demonstrating exactly this flaw, so the pattern is a genuine instance of the vulnerability class even though it's teaching code.

User-supplied `token` request parameter is base64-decoded and fed directly into ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no allow-listing, resolveClass override, or signature verification, matching classic Java deserialization RCE/DoS patterns; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable teaching lesson for exactly this flaw, so it is a genuine instance of the vulnerability class reachable from untrusted input.

fromString() deserializes an arbitrary Base64-decoded byte stream via ObjectInputStream.readObject() with no ObjectInputFilter, allow-list, or type check; if the string parameter originates from user input (as it does in this WebGoat deserialization lesson), this is a classic unauthenticated deserialization gadget-chain vector.

The Cookie object is constructed and only setPath/setSecure are called before response.addCookie(cookie); setHttpOnly(true) is never invoked, so the cookie is sent without the HttpOnly flag, making it readable by client-side scripts (XSS-facilitated session hijacking).

The cookie is constructed and added to the response with setSecure(true) but setHttpOnly is never called, so the code as shown genuinely lacks the HttpOnly flag; this is WebGoat's HijackSessionAssignment lesson, intentionally vulnerable, but the code pattern itself is a real instance of the reported defect class rather than a tool misclassification.

This is WebGoat, an intentionally vulnerable training application, and the file is a lesson (HijackSessionAssignment) whose entire purpose is to demonstrate insecure cookie-based session handling to students; the missing SameSite attribute here is deliberate lesson content rather than a genuine defect in a real production system.

The static `id` field is seeded from `new Random().nextLong()` and is directly used to build authentication/session identifiers via `GENERATE_SESSION_ID`/`AUTHENTICATION_SUPPLIER`, which is exactly the weak-RNG-for-security-token pattern the rule flags; `java.util.Random` is predictable and unsuitable for session/auth ID generation, so `SecureRandom` should be used instead.

The JWT signing secret is selected via `new Random()` (non-cryptographic PRNG) from a tiny 5-element wordlist, then used as JWTSecretKeyEndpoint.JWT_SECRET to sign tokens — this is a textbook weak-RNG-for-security-token defect (trivially brute-forceable secret space of 5), matching CWE-330 exactly even though it's WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable teaching example.

The handler only builds and returns a JWT string with no state-changing side effects (no writes to session, DB, or shared mutable state visible), so CSRF is not applicable even though the HTTP method is unrestricted; this is also a WebGoat training lesson endpoint intentionally exposing a weak secret, not a genuine app defect.

The Cookie holding the signed JWT access_token is created and added to the response without ever calling setSecure(true) (or setHttpOnly), so the token can be transmitted over plain HTTP and is exposed to interception/theft; nothing in the shown code mitigates this.

The cookie carries a signed JWT access token and is added via response.addCookie without ever calling cookie.setHttpOnly(true), so it is genuinely readable by client-side scripts, matching the missing-HttpOnly defect class exactly as shown.

The cookie carrying the signed JWT access_token is created and added to the response via addCookie() without ever calling setSecure(true), so it will be transmitted over plain HTTP, exposing the auth token to network interception.

The 'access_token' cookie containing a signed JWT is created with `new Cookie(...)` and added via `response.addCookie(cookie)` without ever calling `setHttpOnly(true)`, so client-side scripts (e.g., via XSS) could read the auth token, matching the rule exactly.

The code creates the auth cookie via `new Cookie(...)` and adds it via `response.addCookie(cookie)` without ever setting the SameSite attribute (or using a cookie/header API that defaults it), and there is no CSRF token check on this state-setting GET endpoint that issues a session/JWT auth cookie. This matches the rule's pattern exactly: cookie-based auth without SameSite defense-in-depth against CSRF.

The code creates an 'access_token' cookie carrying a signed JWT and adds it to the response via response.addCookie(cookie) without ever calling cookie.setSecure(true) or cookie.setHttpOnly(true), so the session token could be transmitted over plain HTTP, matching the missing-secure-flag pattern exactly as shown.

The access_token cookie (both here and on the success path at line 120) is created without calling setSecure(true), so it can be transmitted over plaintext HTTP; this is a genuine missing-Secure-flag defect on a security-relevant cookie, even though the specific flagged instance carries an empty value.

The access_token cookie (a JWT auth token, set identically at line 120 and cleared here at line 125-126) is created via `new Cookie(...)` and added with `response.addCookie(cookie)` without ever calling `setHttpOnly(true)`, so the browser will allow client-side scripts to read this authentication cookie via document.cookie, enabling token theft through XSS.

The cookie is created and added via response.addCookie() without ever calling setSecure(true) or setHttpOnly(true), so it will be transmitted over plain HTTP; this holds for both the token-bearing cookie (line 121) and the cleared cookie (line 126).

The Cookie object added to the response at line 126 never calls setHttpOnly(true), so the cookie is genuinely missing the HttpOnly attribute in reachable production-lesson code; impact is reduced because this particular cookie carries an empty value (used to clear the session on auth failure) rather than the actual token, but the flagged code pattern itself is a real defect, and the sibling cookie on line 120-121 (the real JWT) has the same missing flag.

The code sets an authentication cookie (access_token) via the plain javax.servlet Cookie API with no SameSite attribute configured anywhere in the shown code, and there is no visible CSRF token check on the corresponding endpoints, so cookie-based auth here is exploitable via cross-site requests.

Both Cookie objects (the token-bearing cookie at line 120 and the cleared cookie at line 125) are added to the response without ever calling setSecure(true), so the code as shown will transmit the access_token cookie over plain HTTP if available, matching the missing-secure-flag rule.

The `jku` value comes directly from an attacker-controlled, unverified JWT header (decoded before signature verification) and is passed straight into `new URL(...)` to fetch JWKs with no allowlist or validation, letting an attacker point the server at an arbitrary host — this is precisely the intentional SSRF vulnerability WebGoat's JWT JKU lesson teaches.

The endpoint takes a raw user-supplied 'url' request parameter and passes it directly into ModelAndView's redirect: prefix with no validation, allowlisting, or sanitization, allowing arbitrary open redirects; the code comment even confirms this is intentional and unvalidated.

The reflected "answer" value is looked up from a fixed server-side questions map via `questions.get(question)`, not built from attacker-supplied content; only the map key is influenced by the request, so unless the map's hardcoded values themselves contain script (not shown), there is no injectable payload reaching the output.

The traversal guard only inspects the raw, still-URL-encoded query string for literal '..' or '/'; request.getParameter("id") returns the decoded value, so an attacker can encode a payload (e.g. %2e%2e%2f) to bypass the check and still get a traversal-controlled path passed into new File(catPicturesDirectory, id+".jpg"), which is then read and returned to the client.

The filter checks the raw, undecoded queryString for '..' and '/', but request.getParameter("id") returns the URL-decoded value used to build the File path, so an attacker can send a URL-encoded id (e.g. %2e%2e%2f) to bypass the check and traverse outside catPicturesDirectory, and the file contents are returned to the client via exists()/copyToByteArray.

The HTML string is built from strength.getFeedback().getWarning()/getSuggestions() and getScore(), which are canned/static messages and a numeric score generated by the zxcvbn password-strength library based on its own predefined feedback catalog, not the raw user-supplied password text reflected verbatim. Without evidence that arbitrary user input is echoed into this output, this doesn't demonstrate an exploitable XSS sink.

The output string is built from `strength.getScore()` (a numeric score) and zxcvbn's canned feedback suggestion strings, not from directly reflected user-supplied text; zxcvbn suggestions come from a fixed set of advisory messages rather than echoing the raw password, so there's no attacker-controlled payload reaching the HTML sink shown here.

The cookie here is only used to clear an existing cookie (empty value, maxAge 0) during logout/cleanup, so there is no sensitive data transmitted regardless of the Secure flag; missing setSecure on an empty, immediately-expiring cookie poses no meaningful confidentiality risk.

This cookie is only used to clear/delete the SpoofCookie value (empty string, maxAge 0), so even without HttpOnly there is no sensitive session data exposed to client-side script; it's a benign cleanup endpoint in a WebGoat teaching lesson, not a real information-disclosure risk.

The cookie is added via response.addCookie(cookie) without calling setSecure(true), so it will be transmitted over plain HTTP; this is a WebGoat lesson file but the code itself genuinely lacks the secure flag, matching the rule's exact class of defect.

The code constructs a Cookie and adds it to the response without ever calling setHttpOnly(true), which matches the rule's pattern exactly; however, since this specific cookie is used only to clear the session (empty value, maxAge=0), the practical exploitability/impact is low, tempering confidence.

This is a WebGoat teaching-lesson endpoint that merely clears a spoofable demo cookie by setting maxAge=0; there is no authenticated state change or sensitive action being protected, so even though the cookie lacks SameSite, a forged request here has no meaningful security impact.

This cookie carries an empty value with maxAge(0), i.e. it's a deletion/cleanup cookie with no sensitive content to protect; while the secure flag is technically absent, there is no exploitable confidentiality impact from missing it here since nothing meaningful is transmitted.

The Cookie object is constructed and added to the response without ever calling setHttpOnly(true), so client-side scripts can read this authentication cookie, matching the missing-HttpOnly defect class exactly.

The code constructs a Cookie, sets Path and Secure, but never calls setHttpOnly(true) before response.addCookie(newCookie), so the cookie is readable via client-side JavaScript, matching CWE-1004 exactly.

The cookie is created and added to the response without ever setting a SameSite attribute (only setPath and setSecure are called), and it is used as an authentication token (COOKIE_NAME/EncDec.encode(username)), so if consumed cross-site it lacks the SameSite defense-in-depth against CSRF. Note this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable 'SpoofCookie' lesson, so the weak cookie handling here is itself the pedagogical point rather than an accidental production bug, but the code as shown does match

The `username` request parameter flows unsanitized into a string-concatenated SQL query executed via `Statement.executeQuery`, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g. `username_reg=' OR '1'='1`); note the very next block correctly uses a PreparedStatement, confirming the reported line is the actual unsafe path.

The query string is built by direct concatenation of the untrusted `username` request parameter into a SQL statement executed via `Statement.executeQuery`, with no sanitization or parameterization, making it classic SQL injection; the file being a WebGoat vulnerable-by-design lesson doesn't change that the code itself is a genuine instance of the vulnerable pattern.
username comes directly from @RequestParam and is concatenated unsanitized into the SQL string, then run via Statement.executeQuery, a classic SQL injection sink reachable from untrusted HTTP input.

The username parameter, taken directly from an HTTP request, is concatenated unsanitized into a SQL query string executed via Statement.executeQuery, allowing SQL injection (e.g., closing the quote to alter the WHERE clause); this is exactly the pattern the neighboring PreparedStatement code avoids.

The `username` parameter is taken directly from user input via @RequestParam and concatenated into the SQL string without sanitization, then executed via Statement.executeQuery, a classic SQL injection pattern (note the contrast with the properly parameterized INSERT just below it).

The code genuinely calls sqle.printStackTrace() in a catch block that swallows the exception, exactly matching the debug-code-left-in pattern the rule targets; this is real, unsanitized debug output in a live code path, not a test/mock file.

The code calls e.printStackTrace() in a catch-all Exception handler with a 'do nothing' comment, matching the rule's exact target pattern of leftover debug code; while the stack trace itself isn't returned to the client (only the password field is), printStackTrace is a genuine anti-pattern that can leak sensitive info into logs/console instead of being handled or logged properly.

The query string is built via direct concatenation of the untrusted `action` parameter into a SQL LIKE clause and executed with a plain java.sql.Statement (not PreparedStatement), with no sanitization or escaping visible in this method — this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable SQL injection lesson, demonstrating the exact CWE-89 pattern.

The query string is built via direct concatenation of the untrusted 'action' parameter into a LIKE clause and executed with Statement.executeQuery instead of a PreparedStatement, allowing SQL injection; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson demonstrating exactly this flaw.

The `query` parameter comes directly from an unauthenticated @RequestParam and is passed unmodified into `statement.executeQuery(query)` using a plain java.sql.Statement rather than a PreparedStatement, with no sanitization or validation visible in the shown code — this is a genuine SQL injection sink (intentionally so, as this is a WebGoat SQL injection training lesson, but the code itself is truly vulnerable).

User-controlled parameters (account, operator, injection) are concatenated directly into a SQL string with no sanitization and executed via Statement.executeQuery, which is exactly the classic SQL injection pattern this WebGoat lesson is designed to teach.

The executed statement (line 69) is a PreparedStatement with the parameter already bound via query.setInt(1, count) on line 65; the queryString.replace(...) concatenation on lines 57-61 only builds a human-readable feedback/error message and is never passed to executeQuery(), so the actual query execution is parameterized and not subject to injection.

The query string concatenates user-controlled `name` and `auth_tan` directly into SQL text with no sanitization or parameterization, then executes it via Statement.executeQuery — a textbook SQL injection sink; using PreparedStatement with bind parameters would fix it.

User-controlled 'name' and 'auth_tan' values are directly concatenated into a SQL string and executed via Statement.executeQuery without any sanitization or parameterization, which is a textbook SQL injection pattern (this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable SQL injection teaching lesson, confirming the code is genuinely exploitable as shown).

The query is built via string concatenation of an unsanitized 'action' parameter and executed with a plain Statement instead of a PreparedStatement; the only mitigation is replacing single quotes with double quotes, which is easily bypassed (e.g. via backslash-escaping or DB-specific quoting) and does not constitute proper parameterization.

The query is built via raw string concatenation and executed with a plain Statement rather than a PreparedStatement; the attempted sanitization only replaces single quotes with double quotes, which is incomplete (e.g., backslash-based escape sequences in MySQL can still break out of the string), so untrusted `action` input can still inject SQL.

queryInjection is built via string concatenation (including auth_tan, visible at lines 53-55) and executed directly with statement.execute(), a classic SQL injection sink with no parameterization; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson code demonstrating the exact CWE-89 pattern reachable from user-supplied input.

The query string is built by concatenating unsanitized input (auth_tan) directly into SQL text and executed via Statement.execute() rather than a PreparedStatement with bound parameters, which is the canonical SQL injection pattern; this is WebGoat's intentionally vulnerable lesson code demonstrating exactly this flaw.

getSqlInt executes a raw Statement.executeQuery(query) rather than a PreparedStatement, which is a genuine anti-pattern, but the only call sites shown (getMaxSalary, getSumSalariesOfOtherEmployees) pass hardcoded constant strings with no user input; whether other callers pass untrusted/concatenated input isn't visible in this snippet.

The `column` parameter comes directly from an unvalidated @RequestParam and is concatenated into the SQL string's ORDER BY clause; wrapping it in prepareStatement does not parameterize it since it's string-concatenated before being passed, allowing SQL injection via the column value.

errors is built from compiler diagnostic messages (d.getMessage(null)) which can echo back fragments of the student-submitted source code, concatenated with raw "
" and returned via .output() in a ResponseBody; since "
" is manually embedded, the output is clearly rendered as HTML client-side without encoding, so attacker-controlled text reflected in a compiler error (e.g. via a string literal in the submitted code) could inject markup/script.

The code contains a literal exception.printStackTrace() call left in an exception handler, which is exactly the debug-code pattern CWE-489 targets; this dumps stack traces to stderr in production code rather than using proper logging/error handling. Severity is modest since output goes to stderr (not directly to the HTTP response) and this is WebGoat teaching-app code, but it still matches the rule's defect class precisely.

The code directly calls e.printStackTrace(), which writes stack traces to stderr and is a genuine instance of active debug code left in a request-handling path; it also leaks e.getMessage() to the HTTP response, compounding the information-exposure risk exactly as the rule describes.

field1 is untrusted user input concatenated unescaped into an HTML string (cart) that is returned via .output(), which WebGoat renders as HTML in the response — this is a genuine reflected-XSS sink pattern, even though it's intentionally vulnerable lesson code by design.

Line 82 just builds a success response with hardcoded feedback keys and cart.toString(); field1 (the tainted input) is only used in a pattern-match check (XSS_PATTERN.test), not reflected into the response body shown here, so there's no visible sink for untrusted HTML.

This is WebGoat's reflected-XSS lesson (CrossSiteScriptingLesson5a), and cart.toString() reflects user-controlled cart contents directly into the response output field with no encoding shown, matching the same unsanitized pattern used in the success paths (lines 79, 84) — this is the intentional vulnerable sink the lesson is built around, though without seeing the client-side rendering of the 'output' field, full end-to-end exploitability can't be confirmed from this snippet alone.

webGoatHomeDirectory is combined with an unsanitized username (set at account creation, not shown here to be restricted to safe characters) to construct a file path that is then opened for writing; if username can contain path separators or traversal sequences, an attacker can write files outside the intended /XXE/ directory.

The endpoint only returns a static, hardcoded DTD string with no side effects or state changes, so CSRF is not applicable even though the HTTP method is unspecified; missing method restriction is a hardening gap, not an exploitable CSRF vector here.

The unmapped-method @RequestMapping only backs a read-only getter that returns a configured file-location string with no state change, side effect, or authorization-sensitive action, so CSRF exploitation of this endpoint has no meaningful impact even though the HTTP method is unrestricted.

The flagged line 79 only constructs a File object for a debug log message; it performs no filesystem read/write and cannot itself be exploited for path traversal. The actual file write with the tainted originalFilename occurs earlier at line 75 via Files.copy, which is the real (unflagged) sink here.

The flagged line 79 only constructs a File object to pass to log.debug() for a debug message; it performs no filesystem read/write/access, so there is no exploitable sink at this location. The actual file write with the unsanitized originalFilename happens earlier at line 75 via Files.copy/resolve, but that is not the reported line.

This is vendored third-party Ace editor code implementing the classic postMessage-based setZeroTimeout trick; the message payload is only an internal, non-secret correlation token (messageName) and the listener checks e.data equality before acting, so no sensitive data is disclosed regardless of the wildcard target origin.

The regex is built from `component`, an internal module-type string (e.g. "worker") derived from module path splitting inside the Ace editor's own module-loading logic, not from arbitrary attacker-controlled input; the pattern itself is also a simple non-backtracking alternation of escaped-literal delimiters, not susceptible to catastrophic backtracking.

This is vendored third-party code (ace.js editor library) where `component` is an internal module identifier (e.g. "worker", "snippets") passed by the calling code, not untrusted user input; additionally the constructed pattern `^X[\-_]|[\-_]X$` has no nested quantifiers or overlapping alternation, so it cannot exhibit catastrophic backtracking regardless of input.

This is vendored third-party Ace editor library code building a regex from tokenizer `rule.regex` values defined in syntax-highlighting mode definitions, not from direct untrusted HTTP/user input; exploitability would require the embedding application to expose arbitrary custom highlighting-rule regexes to attackers, which isn't shown here.

This is vendored Ace editor library code building a regex from its own tokenizer rule definitions (src) with a flag string; there's no shown path from untrusted external input into src/flag, and the pattern is Ace's standard internal regex-builder used across all its bundled modes, not application-specific attacker-reachable code.

This is vendored third-party Ace editor library code implementing its own regex-normalization helper (used to build tokenizer/search regexes internally), not custom application logic; the non-literal RegExp construction here is core library functionality rather than a demonstrable ReDoS vector reachable from untrusted network input in WebGoat's app code.

This is vendored third-party code (Ace editor's tokenizer/lexer) where `src` is built from statically-defined language-mode grammar rules, not from live user/network input; the shown context has no untrusted-input path reaching this constructor, so it's a generic vendored-library lint hit rather than an exploitable ReDoS sink.

The RegExp is built from `type`, a token-type string derived from the editor's own tokenizer (already matched against /^comment|string/ and concatenated with a fixed literal), not from arbitrary attacker-controlled input; it's vendored ace.js editor code with no path for injecting a malicious regex pattern, so ReDoS is not realistically reachable here.

The RegExp is built from `type`, a token-type string produced internally by the tokenizer/lexer (constrained to values like "comment", "string", or "comment|doc-start"), not from user- or attacker-controlled input; this is vendored ace.js editor library code with no untrusted data flow into the regex construction.

The regex is built from `token.type`, an internal tokenizer classification string from Ace's own lexer (not raw untrusted user input), and is only lightly transformed via fixed `.replace()` calls; this is vendored third-party editor library code, not application logic processing external input.

This is vendored ace.js editor code; the regex is built from `token.type`, an internal tokenizer classification string produced by the editor's own lexer, not attacker-controlled network input, so there's no realistic ReDoS attack surface here.

This is vendored third-party Ace editor library code, not application logic; the regex is built from token.type (an internal tokenizer classification string like 'lparen' or 'start') rather than arbitrary user input, and the resulting pattern is short and non-catastrophic (simple alternation, no nested quantifiers), so there's no realistic ReDoS vector here.

typeRe is only constructed here when not already supplied, from token.type — an internal tokenizer classification string from Ace's own bounded vocabulary of token types, not free-form user input — and the resulting pattern `(\.?type)+` has no nested quantifiers or ambiguous alternation that would cause catastrophic backtracking.

This is vendored Ace editor library code building a regex from the user's own search-box input (needle) to power find/replace in the editor the same user is typing into; there's no cross-trust-boundary attacker input, and any ReDoS would only self-DoS the searching user's own browser tab, not a shared server or other users' sessions.

This is vendored third-party code (Ace editor's libs/ace.js) implementing the editor's own find/search feature; `needle` is the search pattern typed by the local user into their own editor's search box and `modifier` is just regex flags, not attacker-supplied input from a remote/untrusted source, so any ReDoS would be self-inflicted on the user's own browser tab rather than an exploitable defect in application logic.

This is vendored Ace editor library code implementing find/replace regex search; `needle` is the user's own search-box input executed client-side in their own browser tab, so any ReDoS only hangs the searching user's session, not a shared server or other users, and this is expected/intended functionality of a code editor rather than an injectable attacker-controlled sink.

This is jQuery UI's internal widget `_setOption`/`option()` API for setting nested option keys via dot notation; the `key` parameter is normally supplied by plugin/application code, not directly by untrusted external input, and no caller shown here passes attacker-controlled strings into this function. Being vendored third-party library code (jquery-ui-1.10.4.js) rather than application logic, and lacking demonstrated taint propagation to `key`, this looks like a generic pattern match on a loop-

The input term is passed through escapeRegex() (line 7168) which escapes all regex metacharacters before being used to construct the RegExp, so the resulting pattern is effectively a literal string match with no possibility of catastrophic backtracking or regex injection.

The user-controlled `term` is passed through `escapeRegex()` before being used to build the RegExp, which escapes all regex metacharacters (`[\-\[\]{}()*+?.,\\^$|#\s]`); this reduces the pattern to a literal-match search with no attacker-controlled quantifiers/groups, so ReDoS via a malicious pattern is not possible. This is the standard, safe jQuery UI autocomplete filter implementation.

This is bundled third-party jQuery UI library code, not application code, and the dynamic portion is a single escaped keystroke character (1-2 chars) concatenated into a fixed prefix-match pattern "^..." — there's no way for such a short, escaped, non-attacker-supplied-pattern string to cause catastrophic backtracking or ReDoS.

The regex is built from `escape(character)` where character is a single character derived from one keydown event's keyCode, so the resulting pattern is always a fixed-length literal prefix match with no attacker-controlled repetition/quantifiers—there is no possible catastrophic backtracking. This is vetted third-party jQuery UI library code (autocomplete filter), not custom logic processing arbitrary untrusted strings.

This is vendored third-party ace.js editor library code; `commentRegex` is supplied only by internal language-mode definitions (hardcoded start/end markers for languages like Java), not by untrusted end-user input, so there is no attacker-controlled path to this RegExp construction.

This is vendored third-party Ace editor library code; commentRegex is an internal configuration object passed by other language-mode definitions within the same library, not untrusted external input, so there's no attacker-controlled ReDoS path here.

nodeTypes is an internal array of DOM node-type constants passed by callers within this vendored WYSIHTML5 library, not attacker-controlled user input; the resulting regex is short and simple (alternation of small numeric/name tokens) with no exponential backtracking risk.

className here is an internal API parameter of a DOM class-name utility (addClass/removeClass/hasClass) in a vendored third-party library, not shown to originate from untrusted network input; the regex itself has no nested-quantifier structure that would cause catastrophic backtracking regardless of className's value.
